data.title: EU Scrutinizes Google, YouTube, Apple, Snapchat Over Children's Online Safety
data.description:
By Edith Hancock 




  The European Union is looking into how well Alphabet's Google and YouTube, Apple and Snapchat protect minors online under the bloc's content-moderation law. 



  The executive arm of the EU, the European Commission, has asked for information on the companies' age-verification systems and on how they prevent minors from accessing products like drugs and vapes or from seeing harmful material such as content that could encourage eating disorders. 



  "We will do what it takes to ensure the physical and mental wellbeing of children and teens online," Henna Virkkunen, the EU's top tech enforcer, said in a statement Friday. 



  The watchdog asked Snapchat for information about how it prevents children younger than 13 from accessing its services and on the features it has in place to prevent the sale of illegal goods for children. It is also scrutinizing YouTube's age-assurance system and algorithms after reports of harmful content being disseminated to minors, and asking Apple and Google how they manage the risk of users being able to download harmful apps. 



  The EU's Digital Services Act requires companies like Google and Apple to do more to prevent minors being exposed to harmful and illegal content online. Companies can receive fines of up to 6% of their annual worldwide turnover for breaching the regulations. 



  "We have received the request from the commission and will collaborate to provide the necessary information," a Snapchat spokesperson said. 



  While requests for information are a routine part of the commission's law enforcement, officials can later open formal investigations into companies if they suspect breaches of the DSA. 



  Google said the company has worked with child-development experts to build age-appropriate experiences across Google, including on YouTube, with robust controls for parents. "We'll continue to engage with the commission on this critical area," it said. 



  Apple didn't immediately respond to a request for comment. 





  Write to Edith Hancock at edith.hancock@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  October 10, 2025 08:42 ET (12:42 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Canada Unemployment Rate Holds at 7.1% in September
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart 




  OTTAWA--Unemployment in Canada held steady in September as hiring rebounded strongly following two straight months of heavy job losses. 



  Statistics Canada reported Friday that Canada's economy added a net 60,400 jobs last month. That was much stronger than the modest 5,000 rise economists had expected, and partially erases the 106,300 drop in employment over July and August. 



  The unemployment rate was unchanged at 7.1%, where expectations were for the jobless rate to tick up to 7.2%. 



  When calculated using U.S. Labor Department methodology, Canada's unemployment rate last month was unmoved at 6.0%. 





  Write to Robb M. Stewart robb.stewart@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  October 10, 2025 08:46 ET (12:46 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Venture Global Shares Sink After BP Wins -2-
data.description:
Crude Oil Prices Could Be Near Bottom of Cycle -- Market Talk

0316 GMT - There is risk for further weakness in crude prices, ANZ Research strategists Daniel Hynes and Soni Kumari say in a note. They expect lower refinery activity and higher OPEC+ output to lift global oil inventories. A dip below $60/bbl in 4Q wouldn't surprise, they add, though any drop will likely be limited as markets have priced in inventory buildup. For next year, stockpiles outside China are relatively low, which could offset the impact of higher OPEC+ supply, the strategists note. ANZ expects crude at $60-$65/bbl through 1H 2026. A recovery to $70 by end-2026 is possible on a recovery in demand or OPEC production cuts. "Ultimately, we think we are near the bottom of the cycle," they say. Front-month Brent was recently around $65.26/bbl. (colin.ng@wsj.com)

--

BP's U.S. LNG Arbitration Win Boosts Its Turnaround Efforts -- Market Talk

0721 GMT - An unexpected win in arbitration proceedings against U.S. LNG-supplier Venture Global adds another positive element to BP's turnaround efforts, Citi analysts write. BP--and others--have accused Venture Global of withholding contracted cargoes of LNG after the Russia-Ukraine war, and instead selling them on the spot market. Disclosures suggest BP will seek more than $1 billion in damages, the analysts write. It is surprising that BP appears to have won given Venture Global recently scored a win in a similar case against Shell, they add. While $1 billion isn't a game-changing sum for BP, it sits well with the company's restructuring narrative, deleveraging and exploration success, they add. Shares trade down 0.6% at 2,760 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

--

Gold Rally Signals Mounting Distrust in Current Fiscal, Monetary Order -- Market Talk

0343 GMT - The gold rally this year signals increasing distrust in the existing fiscal and monetary order, Ajay Rajadhyaksha, Barclays global chairman of research, says in a note. The debt loads of four major economies--the U.S., the U.K., France and Japan--are all over 100% of their respective GDP, while their fiscal profiles are still worsening, he says. "Most importantly, there is virtually no political appetite for fiscal consolidation," he adds. Meanwhile, other traditional safe-haven assets, such as the yen and the Swiss franc, are losing some of their appeal. Gold typically rises when economies are teetering or financial markets are collapsing. The yellow metal's recent rally despite healthy financial markets should alert policymakers, he says. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

--

Tesla Best Placed Among U.S. Automakers to Tackle China's Rare-Earth Control -- Market Talk

0320 GMT - Tesla is best positioned among U.S. automakers to navigate China's tighter rare-earth controls, according to Wedbush analysts. "Tesla's massive presence in China on this front is a relative good sign for Musk and co.," the analysts say in a research note. China's tightened grip will have ripple effects for the auto industry, as these raw materials are crucial for producing computer chips, brakes, seats and other critical vehicle components, they add. Automakers have long been trying to reduce their dependence on China for rare earths, but these supply chains haven't been fully integrated, the analysts note. "The bark could be worse than bite," however, as China continues to use such tactics to gain leverage in trade negotiations, they say. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

--

Indonesia's Biodiesel Blend Mandate Might Support Palm Oil Demand, Prices -- Market Talk

0317 GMT - Indonesia's planned rollout of the 50% biodiesel blend mandate by 2H 2026 is expected to tighten palm oil supply and boost crude palm oil prices, CIMB Securities analyst Ivy Ng Lee Fang says in a note. The policy, aimed at reducing diesel imports and strengthening energy security, could generate an additional four million tons of CPO demand, she says. While feedstock constraints might prompt a 45% blend mandate initially, the full 50% blend implementation would be structurally positive for planters. CIMB maintains its overweight rating on Malaysia's agriculture-and-forestry sector, with SD Guthrie, IOI Corp., TA Ann, and Hap Seng Plantations as top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

--

Iron Ore Rises as Markets Focus on Chinese Steel Export Limits -- Market Talk

0252 GMT - Iron ore rises in early Asian trade with markets subdued given the recent Chinese holidays. Markets will likely be concerned about any further efforts to limit China's steel exports, ANZ Research strategists Daniel Hynes and Soni Kumari say. China accounts for about 37% of Europe's steel imports, and with European officials reportedly considering doubling tariffs on certain imports to 50%, combined with Beijing's crackdown on overcapacity, a reduction in output is expected. Given weak domestic demand, "any further constraints to steel exports could lead to weakness in demand for steelmaking raw materials, such as iron ore," the strategists say in a note. The contract for January delivery on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is up 1.0% at 800.5 yuan a ton. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

--

Strong Catalysts Might Continue to Support Gold Prices -- Market Talk

0250 GMT - Strong catalysts could continue to support gold prices, which have powered past the $4,000 mark, shrugging off dollar strength, Alexandra Symeonidi, corporate credit analyst at William Blair, says. Investor and central bank demand has remained resilient despite added costs to further accumulate gold, she says. Central banks returned to buying gold in August after a brief pause in July, she notes. However, despite strong gold prices, platinum remains the best-performing metal across precious and base metals, with gains of over 80% year to date, while silver crossed $50 for the first time in decades, she says. (monica.gupta@wsj.com)

--

Copper Lower Due to Profit-Taking -- Market Talk

0120 GMT - Copper is lower in early Asian trade due to profit-taking. The base metal's recent over-a-year high has come amid supply disruption worries. Copper prices might face short-term corrections, but the medium-to-long-term outlook remains bullish, Nanhua Futures' analysts say in a commentary. The three-month LME copper contract is 0.7% lower at $10,797.00 a ton. (tracy.qu@wsj.com) 




  Write to Barcelona editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  October 10, 2025 08:35 ET (12:35 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Venture Global Shares Sink After BP Wins Arbitration Case Over LNG Dispute — Commodities Roundup
data.description:
MARKET MOVEMENTS:

--Brent crude oil is down 1.5% to $64.26 a barrel

--European benchmark gas is down 1.2% to 31.96 euros a megawatt-hour

--Gold futures are up 0.8% to $4,003 a troy ounce

--LME three-month copper futures are down 1.1% to $10,751.50 a metric ton 




  TOP STORY: 



  Venture Global Shares Sink After BP Wins Arbitration Case Over LNG Dispute 



  Shares in Venture Global tumbled after the U.S. natural-gas exporter suffered a surprising loss in an arbitration case against customer BP. 



  The stock fell 19% to $10.25 in premarket trading Friday. Shares had their public debut in January and opened trading at $24.05 apiece, down 3.8% from their $25 IPO price. 





  OTHER STORIES: 



  Aluminum Could Become the Next Copper 



  Metals are having a moment. Aluminum could be the next to shine. 



  Gold breached $4,000 an ounce this week and silver neared a record high, while platinum is up roughly 80% so far this year. Beyond the precious metals, industrial metals like copper have been strong performers for the past few years. Aluminum, despite its many useful properties, has been largely overlooked. But that could be about to change. 



  -- 



  Why Gold Will Lose Its Luster 



  Investors' latest shiny object is an actual shiny object. 



  The price of gold has beaten U.S. stocks, bitcoin and even shares of AI wonder-stock Nvidia this year. There's no saying how far that epic run will go, but there are almost certainly better places to park your savings in the long run. History and common sense tell us so. 





  MARKET TALKS: 



  Gold Rises 1% After Slight Pullback -- Market Talk 



  1138 GMT - Gold prices regain ground in afternoon trade after pulling back slightly earlier in the session, with futures in New York rising 1% to $4,012.30 a troy ounce. "Dip-buyers re-emerged around $3,945, keeping the $4,000 area well defended for now," Daniela Sabin Hathorn, senior analyst at Capital.com, says. "One near-term headwind to watch is dollar firmness; a stronger USD can cap the upside in gold even if the macro backdrop remains supportive." On Friday, the U.S. dollar index--which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies--is 0.2% lower at 99.36. Meanwhile, silver futures climb 2.8% to $48.49 an ounce, while platinum is up 0.3% at $1,661.90 an ounce. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  Oil Down on Easing Geopolitical Risks, Oversupply Prospects 



  1128 GMT - Oil prices extend losses in afternoon trade after Israel said its troops had begun withdrawing from parts of Gaza as a cease-fire opened a 72-hour window for the release of hostages. A lower geopolitical risk premium in the region, along with concerns over a looming supply surplus, have pushed crude benchmarks 1% lower, with Brent and WTI now trading at $64.53 and $60.39 a barrel, respectively. "Our global oil inventory tracker is now pointing to a significant oversupplied oil market, triggered by a sharply increasing oil-in-transit," analysts at DNB Carnegie say. "We believe the sharp increase in oil-in-transit will show up in onshore oil inventories over the next months, and underpin downward pressure on oil prices." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  Copper Near 16-Month Highs on Mounting Fears of Supply Shortages -- Market Talk 



  1115 GMT - Copper prices are broadly stable in afternoon trade but remain near levels last seen more than a year ago as concerns over tightening supply rise across markets. Futures on the London Metal Exchange gain 0.1% to $10,780 a metric ton after settling almost 2% higher in the previous session, when the reopening of China's markets triggered a wave of buying. Recent production disruptions at major mines in Indonesia and Chile have eroded prospects of supply growth, with the International Copper Study Group now forecasting a deficit of 150,000 tons next year from previous estimates of a 209,000-ton surplus. "The supply situation has deteriorated significantly in a very short time," Thu Lan Nguyen from Commerzbank says. Meanwhile, expectations for further interest-rate cuts in the U.S. are also providing support, as lower rates generally lead to increased borrowing and spending, boosting demand. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  Palm Oil Ends Lower Despite Export Growth -- Market Talk 



  1037 GMT - Palm oil closed lower despite export data showing a rise in demand. Malaysia's palm oil exports for Oct. 1-10 likely rose 19% on month to 495,415 metric tons, according to cargo surveyor AmSpec Agri Malaysia. Concerns about tightening supply after Indonesia reaffirmed its plans to roll out a 50% palm oil-based biodiesel blend in 2H 2026 could also lift prices, AmInvestment Bank says in a note. However, price gains might be capped by lower demand from top buyer India, which has sufficient vegetable oil stockpiles at its ports, it adds. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for December delivery fell 45 ringgit to 4,546 ringgit a ton. (jason.chau@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  China's Latest Rare-Earths Export Controls Might Complicate U.S. Trade Talks -- Market Talk 



  1028 GMT - China's expanded export controls on rare earths appear aimed at boosting its leverage in U.S. trade negotiations, Capital Economics says. President Trump and Chinese leader Xi Jinping are due to meet on the sidelines of an APEC summit in South Korea in a few weeks' time, economist Julian Evans-Pritchard says. "While the timing of the [rare earths] move may be opportunistic, we suspect the new controls are mostly motivated by medium-term geostrategic goals," CE says. China's stranglehold on rare earths won't last forever, as deposits outside the country are plentiful. But Beijing could shore up the position of its manufacturing sector and hold back foreign competitors before they manage to reduce reliance on Chinese supply, Evans-Pritchard says. (jason.chau@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  European Gas Falls on Rising Supply, Easing Geopolitical Tensions -- Market Talk 



  0852 GMT - European natural-gas prices fall in mid-morning trading, pressured by signs of rising supply and easing geopolitical risks. The benchmark Dutch TTF is down 1.3% to 31.94 euros a megawatt hour. "Futures fell on reports about the imminent startup of the Golden Pass LNG project in Texas," analysts at ANZ say. "It's one of the biggest export facilities to come online and heralds a wave of supply that threatens to push the market into surplus next year." Meanwhile, weak demand from China has reduced competition across LNG markets, freeing up more cargoes for Europe ahead of the heating season. According to Gas Infrastructure Europe, EU storage levels are currently 82.8% full. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  Gold Pulls Back Below $4,000 But Set for Weekly Gain -- Market Talk 



  0800 GMT - Gold prices slip back beneath the $4,000 mark, but remain on track for an eighth consecutive weekly gain as structural drivers stay in place. Futures in New York rise 0.2% to $3,982.10 a troy ounce in early trade and are up 1.9% on week. The retreat from record highs reflects profit-taking pressures and easing geopolitical risks after Israel signed off on President Trump's plan for a cease-fire and hostage release in Gaza. However, any pullback is expected to be short-lived. The Federal Reserve is still expected to cut interest rates further, supporting non-yielding gold, while the prolonged U.S. government shutdown is injecting new uncertainty into markets. "If the shutdown does persist into next week, we won't get the CPI release on Wednesday, which will leave us increasingly uncertain about the U.S. economy," analysts at Deutsche Bank say. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  Oil Falls as Gaza Deal Eases Geopolitical Risk Premium 



  0741 GMT - Oil prices fall in early trade after Israel approved the U.S.-brokered deal for a cease-fire and hostage release in Gaza. "This presents a major step toward ending the two-year war that raised the risk of supply disruptions in the oil market," analysts at ANZ Research say. Brent crude and WTI are both down 0.5% to $64.88 and $60.76 a barrel, respectively, after settling 1.6% lower in the previous session. Prices are also pressured by concerns over an impending supply glut as OPEC+ continues to boost production and the latest U.S. crude stockpiles data point to weaker demand. However, further losses are limited by new U.S. sanctions on Iran, with the Treasury Department targeting more than 50 individuals, entities and vessels facilitating Iranian oil and liquefied petroleum gas sales and shipments. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  Comex Gold Futures Has Likely Formed Interim Peak at $4,000/oz, Daily Chart Shows -- Market Talk 



  0736 GMT - Comex gold futures has likely formed an interim peak at $4,000/oz, RHB Retail Research's Joseph Chai says in a research report. The commodity closed Thursday with a long bearish candlestick pattern on the daily chart, the analyst notes. Thursday's price action suggests an interim peak has emerged there, Chai says. With negative momentum picking up, the precious metal could pull back toward $3,900/oz, the analyst says. However, overall technical structure is still bullish and the 20-day simple moving average is trending upward to provide additional support. After consolidation, the commodity will probably resume upside movements, Chai adds. Spot gold is 0.3% lower at $3,963.75/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  China's Solar Glass Capacity Set for Temporary Boost in 4Q -- Market Talk 



  0526 GMT - China's solar glass capacity is set to see a temporary boost in the fourth quarter, Deutsche Bank analyst Gary Zhou says in a note. Citing Shanghai Metals Market data, Zhou notes that around 5,000 tons a day of new solar glass capacity could start operation in 4Q, representing roughly 6% of current domestic operating capacity. "The possible supply addition is likely triggered by the recent solar glass price and margin recovery", he adds, with inventories beginning to rise. However, DB expects the recovery, driven by voluntary production curbs, to end soon. Within the solar sub-sectors, the bank favors the polysilicon sector with GCL Technology as its top pick. GCL shares last at HK$1.33. (jason.chau@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  (MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires



  October 10, 2025 08:35 ET (12:35 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: BASF to Sell Majority Stake in Coatings Unit to Carlyle for $6.7 Billion
data.description:
By Adria Calatayud 




  German chemicals giant BASF agreed to sell a majority stake in its auto-coatings business to Carlyle for about 5.8 billion euros ($6.71 billion). 



  BASF said Friday that the deal is valued at 7.7 billion euros including debt. The company will reinvest in the business, keeping a 40% stake and will get 5.8 billion euros in cash when the deal closes, it said. 



  The group had been exploring options for its auto-coatings business for a while, as part of a plan to focus on core operations. 



  BASF said the deal marks a significant step in its strategy. 



  Carlyle, which is acting in partnership with Qatar Investment Authority, said it sees compelling opportunities to support the business becoming an established independent company. 





  Write to Adria Calatayud at adria.calatayud@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  October 10, 2025 08:19 ET (12:19 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: The Chop House to Open New Alcoa Location in Spring 2026
data.description:
The Chop House to Open New Alcoa Location in Spring 2026

Award-winning restaurant group behind Connors Steak & Seafood expands steakhouse portfolio

The Chop House, part of the restaurant group that introduced the award-winning Connors Steak & Seafood to the Southeast, announced plans to open The Chop House in Alcoa, Tennessee, in spring 2026. The expansion underscores The Chop House's continued growth in East Tennessee and its commitment to delivering "Superior Dining Experiences. Every Day."

Scheduled to open in spring 2026, The Chop House Alcoa will feature rich wood décor and comfortable seating that create a warm, inviting atmosphere. The restaurant will offer a menu of hand-cut steaks and chops, fresh seafood prepared in-house, and signature cocktails, continuing the brand's tradition of combining premium offerings with friendly service in a relaxed, upscale-casual environment.

The location will be open daily for lunch beginning at 11 a.m., featuring a thoughtfully curated lunch menu, and will also host compelling happy hours.

The new Alcoa restaurant will create numerous local employment opportunities, with hiring expected to begin in early 2026. Additional details about the location and the specific opening date will be released as the project progresses.

"We're excited to bring The Chop House experience to Alcoa," said Rick Tasman, CEO of Connor Concepts. "East Tennessee holds a special place in our company's history, and this new restaurant represents both our gratitude to the community and our ongoing commitment to growth."

The Connor Concepts restaurant group operates The Chop House and Connors Steak & Seafood locations throughout the Southeast, many of which are recognized by OpenTable among the Top 100 Most Loved Restaurants in America, along with numerous regional honors.

About The Chop House

Founded in 1992, The Chop House is an upscale-casual steakhouse concept featuring hand-cut premium steaks, fresh seafood, and craft cocktails in a sophisticated yet welcoming environment. Part of the Connor Concepts restaurant group, The Chop House has locations across the Southeast and a reputation for exceptional dining experiences built on high-quality ingredients, impeccable service, and meticulous attention to detail. Each location opens at 11 a.m. daily to serve a thoughtfully curated lunch menu.Learn more at www.TheChopHouse.com

About Connors Steak & Seafood

Connors Steak & Seafood is an award-winning restaurant offering premium steaks, fresh U.S.D.C.-inspected seafood, and an extensive wine selection in an elegant yet comfortable atmosphere. Connors has earned national recognition, including OpenTable's Top 100 Most Romantic Restaurants in America and Top 100 Most Loved Restaurants in America, as well as local honors such as Best Upscale Restaurant by the Huntsville Business Journal, Best Steakhouse in the annual Best of Tallahassee Issue of Tallahassee Magazine. www.connorsrestaurant.com

Media Contact:
Connor Concepts
Courtney DeLaura
(865) 805-6177
courtney@connorconcepts.com

data.title: CHINA DRIVES STRONGEST GROWTH IN GLOBAL FACTORY PURCHASING SINCE MID-2022, WHILE NORTH AMERICAN MANUFACTURERS COOL IN SEPTEMBER: GEP GLOBAL SUPPLY CHAIN VOLATILITY INDEX
data.description:
CHINA DRIVES STRONGEST GROWTH IN GLOBAL FACTORY PURCHASING SINCE MID-2022, WHILE NORTH AMERICAN MANUFACTURERS COOL IN SEPTEMBER: GEP GLOBAL SUPPLY CHAIN VOLATILITY INDEX

CHINA DRIVES STRONGEST GROWTH IN GLOBAL FACTORY PURCHASING SINCE MID-2022, WHILE NORTH AMERICAN MANUFACTURERS COOL IN SEPTEMBER: GEP GLOBAL SUPPLY CHAIN VOLATILITY INDEX

PR Newswire

CLARK, N.J., Oct. 10, 2025
  • Chinese manufacturers sharply increased purchasing in September, propelling Asia's supply chains to their busiest level since June 2022.
  • In contrast, tariff-related disruptions and a softer economic outlook curbed North American manufacturing activity.
  • Europe's recovery remains sluggish, with supply chains operating at their weakest since March.

CLARK, N.J., Oct. 10, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- GEP Global Supply Chain Volatility Index — a leading indicator tracking demand conditions, shortages, transportation costs, inventories, and backlogs based on a monthly survey of 27,000 businesses — was little changed in September (-0.38 vs -0.39 in August), indicating that global supply chains are operating below full capacity still.

However, Chinese factories report a strong rise in purchasing, pushing global manufacturing procurement activity up at the fastest rate since mid-2022. This expansion pushed Asia's supply chains to near-full utilization.

In sharp contrast, North American supply chains lost momentum. Manufacturers cited tariff-related delays and growing concerns about the economic outlook, leading many to hold back purchasing and reduce inventory buffers.

Meanwhile, Europe's supply chains remained underused, as manufacturers in Germany, France, and Italy reduced both purchasing and stockpiles. The region's supply-chain activity fell to its weakest level since March, extending its protracted industrial downturn.

"This is the new normal for global companies — higher prices, tariff pressure, and slower growth are here to stay," said John Piatek, Vice President, Consulting, GEP. "For supply chain leaders who've been waiting to see how things settle: this is as stable as it's going to get — it's time to start executing their revised strategies."

Interpreting the data:
Index > 0, supply chain capacity is being stretched. The further above 0, the more stretched supply chains are.
Index < 0, supply chain capacity is being underutilized. The further below 0, the more underutilized supply chains are.

SEPTEMBER REGIONAL KEY FINDINGS

  • ASIA: Factory activity in China picked up in September, with a notable boost in demand driving the strongest rise in input purchasing across Asia for ten months.
  • NORTH AMERICA: Manufacturers displayed a reticence to stockpile further in September, after August's bumper rise due to concerns about the economic outlook, although delivery delays and tariff-related disruptions were reportedly hindrances.
  • EUROPE: Factory purchasing lost momentum in September, with Germany, France and Italy all reporting softer procurement trends, driving the region's respective Index to a six-month low.
  • U.K.: Although the index rose to -0.57, from -0.90, it is still at a level reflecting significant manufacturing weakness across the country.

SEPTEMBER 2025 DETAILED FINDINGS

  • DEMAND: September saw a revival in factory purchasing, which made its strongest gains since June 2022. Asia was central to this uplift, particularly China, as the globe's second-largest economy ramped up buying to facilitate sharper growth in production and sales. Input demand trends were far more constrained in North America and Europe.

  • INVENTORIES: The frequency at which manufacturers across the globe stockpiled due to price or supply fears continued to decrease in September, indicating factory procurement leaders are becoming less concerned about purchase cost inflation or item availability in the near term.

  • MATERIAL SHORTAGES: Our global supply shortages tracker decreased in September, indicating robust item availability. Factories will have little, if any, challenges in sourcing vendors for commodities, components and other intermediate products.

  • LABOR SHORTAGES: Staffing capacity was not a constraint for global manufacturers during September. Reports of backlogs rising due to labor shortages fell further below the long-term average and were the lowest in six months.

  • TRANSPORTATION: Global transportation costs were in line with historically normal levels during September.

For more information, visit www.gep.com/volatility.

Note: Full historical data dating back to January 2005 is available for subscription. Please contact economics@spglobal.com.

The next release of the GEP Global Supply Chain Volatility Index will be 8 a.m. ET, Nov. 12, 2025.

About the GEP Global Supply Chain Volatility Index

The GEP Global Supply Chain Volatility Index is produced by S&P Global and GEP. It is derived from S&P Global's PMI® surveys, sent to companies in over 40 countries, totaling around 27,000 companies. The headline figure is a weighted sum of six sub-indices derived from PMI data, PMI Comments Trackers and PMI Commodity Price & Supply Indicators compiled by S&P Global.

  • A value above 0 indicates that supply chain capacity is being stretched and supply chain volatility is increasing. The further above 0, the greater the extent to which capacity is being stretched.
  • A value below 0 indicates that supply chain capacity is being underutilized, reducing supply chain volatility. The further below 0, the greater the extent to which capacity is being underutilized.

A Supply Chain Volatility Index is also published at a regional level for Europe, Asia, North America and the U.K. For more information about the methodology, click here.

About GEP

GEP® delivers AI-powered procurement and supply chain solutions that help global enterprises become more agile and resilient, operate more efficiently and effectively, gain competitive advantage, boost profitability and increase shareholder value. Fresh thinking, innovative products, unrivaled domain expertise, smart, passionate people — this is how GEP SOFTWARE™, GEP STRATEGY™ and GEP MANAGED SERVICES™ together deliver procurement and supply chain solutions of unprecedented scale, power and effectiveness. Our customers are the world's best companies, including more than 1,000 Fortune 500 and Global 2000 industry leaders who rely on GEP to meet ambitious strategic, financial and operational goals. A leader in multiple Gartner Magic Quadrants, GEP's cloud-native software and digital business platforms consistently win awards and recognition from industry analysts, research firms and media outlets, including Gartner, Forrester, IDC, ISG, and Spend Matters. GEP is also regularly ranked a top procurement and supply chain consulting and strategy firm, and a leading managed services provider by ALM, Everest Group, NelsonHall, IDC, ISG and HFS, among others. Headquartered in Clark, New Jersey, GEP has offices and operations centers across Europe, Asia, Africa and the Americas. To learn more, visit www.gep.com.

About S&P Global

S&P Global (NYSE: SPGI) S&P Global provides essential intelligence. We enable governments, businesses and individuals with the right data, expertise and connected technology so that they can make decisions with conviction. From helping our customers assess new investments to guiding them through ESG and energy transition across supply chains, we unlock new opportunities, solve challenges and accelerate progress for the world. We are widely sought after by many of the world's leading organizations to provide credit ratings, benchmarks, analytics and workflow solutions in the global capital, commodity and automotive markets. With every one of our offerings, we help the world's leading organizations plan for tomorrow, today.

data.title: Connors Steak & Seafood to Open Naples Location at the Beginning of 2026
data.description:
Connors Steak & Seafood to Open Naples Location at the Beginning of 2026

Award-winning steakhouse expands Florida presence with Southwest Florida destination

Connors Steak & Seafood announces plans to open a Naples location in Q1 of 2026, marking the brand's continued growth in Florida and bringing its signature combination of premium steaks, fresh seafood and warm hospitality to Southwest Florida's thriving dining scene.

The Connors Steak & Seafood Naples restaurant will feature roughly 10,000 square feet of the brand's signature upscale-casual atmosphere, blending elegant design with a relaxed dining experience. The menu will include USDA Prime steaks, fresh-caught USDC-inspected seafood, an extensive wine list and craft cocktails, continuing the company’s legacy of “Superior Dining Experiences. Every Day.”

"Naples will be our fourth Connors Steak & Seafood in Florida, joining our family of restaurants in Sarasota, Fort Myers and Tallahassee," Mike Connor, founder and chairman of Connor Concepts. "We're grateful to this community for welcoming us, and we look forward to serving Naples for years to come. At Connors Steak & Seafood, hospitality and community are at the heart of everything we do."

The new restaurant is expected to create approximately 150 jobs in the Naples area, with hiring announcements and an exact opening date to follow later this year. Connors Steak & Seafood currently operates in Tallahassee, Fort Myers, and Sarasota, where the Sarasota restaurant was recently named an OpenTable Top 100 Most Loved Restaurant in America.

Connors Steak & Seafood Naples will open daily at 11 a.m. to serve a thoughtfully curated lunch menu designed for the midday dining experience. The lunch offerings provide guests with the same premium ingredients and expert preparation that define the dinner menu while accommodating the faster pace of a lunch hour.

About Connors Steak & Seafood

Connors Steak & Seafood is an award-winning restaurant offering premium steaks, fresh U.S.D.C.-inspected seafood, and an extensive wine selection in an elegant yet comfortable atmosphere. Connors has earned national recognition, including OpenTable's Top 100 Most Romantic Restaurants in America and Top 100 Most Loved Restaurants in America, as well as local honors such as Best Upscale Restaurant by the Huntsville Business Journal, Best Steakhouse in the annual Best of Tallahassee Issue of Tallahassee Magazine.

www.connorsrestaurant.com

Media Contact:
Connor Concepts
Courtney DeLaura
(865) 805-6177
courtney@connorconcepts.com

data.title: Fifth Third Mortgage Lending is Strategic Growth Driver
data.description:
Fifth Third Mortgage Lending is Strategic Growth Driver

Mortgage Customers Are 31% More Likely to Stay — Reinforcing Lending’s Role in Long-Term Growth

Fifth Third (NASDAQ: FITB) is opening more doors for homeowners than ever — with smarter mortgage solutions, faster processes, and a customer-first mindset. In 2025 alone, the Bank has originated over $5.2 billion in mortgages, ranking among the top 45 lenders nationally and the top 15 banks. Already a top ten mortgage servicer in the U.S., Fifth Third has increased market share in 90% of its key markets in 2025 — and continues to accelerate its strength and scale in mortgage banking. As Fifth Third prepares for its next chapter of growth, mortgage continues to serve as a cornerstone of customer relationships and regional expansion.

This press release features multimedia. View the full release here: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20251010141802/en/

James Sias, Head of Mortgage Lending and Indirect Dealer Services at Fifth Third onstage with Diego Sanchez, President of HW Media, at HousingWire's Mortgage Banking Summit. Photography by Wes Ellis.

This week, at HousingWire’s inaugural Mortgage Banking Summit, James Sias, Head of Mortgage Lending and Indirect Dealer Services, remarked: “Mortgage isn’t just a product — it’s the ultimate relationship builder. It’s how we become the one bank our customers most value and trust for life.”

Fifth Third’s commitment to mortgage is backed by data. According to Fifth Third’s internal analysis of new mortgage originations from 2019 through 2023, new households with mortgages are 24% more likely to stay with the Bank than similar checking-only households. In the most recent 2023–2024 cohort, that advantage grew to 31%, reinforcing mortgage as a powerful anchor in the customer relationship.

Fifth Third’s focus on building deep, long-lasting customer relationships — with essential products, personnel, and customer experiences — is driving sustainable organic growth. As Fifth Third expands, particularly across the South, mortgages have helped attract, retain and deepen new households. Over the past 24 months, the Bank has attracted hundreds of millions in incremental deposits through its mortgage relationship pricing program, while advising customers holistically across their full financial picture. The Bank is actively hiring Mortgage Loan Officers in new markets and currently offers more than 35 mortgage products tailored to a wide range of income levels, credit profiles, and geographies.

Fifth Third’s relationship approach to mortgages further evolved under the leadership of Jay Plum. In 2023, Jay Plum was named Head of Consumer Lending and brought in industry veterans James Sias, Chad Powers, and Martin Muff to lead the next chapter of mortgage, home equity and servicing. Plum’s customer-centric philosophy, that “every mortgage customer is a family, not a file,” has taken root across the organization, inspiring new first-time homebuyer education programs, more personalized service, and affordable lending solutions designed to reduce friction and build customer confidence.

“What drew me to Fifth Third wasn’t just where it was — but where it could go,” said Plum. “I saw the potential to build on a solid foundation and help shape something exceptional — and that’s exactly what we’re doing. With the momentum we’ve built and the opportunities ahead, we’re excited about what’s next.”

Under Plum’s leadership, the Bank grew mortgage volume 16% from 2023 to 2024, with a 39% increase in retail and direct. Customer satisfaction also climbed, with internal Net Promoter Scores consistently ranging from 74-83 over the last six months.

Beyond traditional lending, Fifth Third is also innovating in home equity and estate planning. In 2025, demand for Fifth Third’s home equity lending products has been extraordinary, with year-over-year volume up 60%. Fifth Third is also a leader in equitable housing, offering free wills to help families protect their homes and preserve generational wealth. Through its innovative Neighborhood Program, Fifth Third has invested nearly $255 million in economic development across ten neighborhoods — catalyzing transformative change and leading to more housing opportunities across the Bank’s footprint.

At Fifth Third, mortgage isn’t just a business — it’s a key pillar in building life-long banking relationships and helping customers realize their dreams of homeownership.

About Fifth Third

Fifth Third is a bank that’s as long on innovation as it is on history. Since 1858, we’ve been helping individuals, families, businesses and communities grow through smart financial services that improve lives. Our list of firsts is extensive, and it’s one that continues to expand as we explore the intersection of tech-driven innovation, dedicated people and focused community impact. Fifth Third is one of the few U.S.-based banks to have been named among Ethisphere’s World’s Most Ethical Companies® for several years. With a commitment to taking care of our customers, employees, communities and shareholders, our goal is not only to be the nation’s highest performing regional bank, but to be the bank people most value and trust.

Fifth Third Bank, National Association is a federally chartered institution. Fifth Third Bancorp is the indirect parent company of Fifth Third Bank and its common stock is traded on the NASDAQ® Global Select Market under the symbol "FITB." Investor information and press releases can be viewed at www.53.com. Deposit and credit products provided by Fifth Third Bank, National Association. Member FDIC.

Sophie Isherwood (Media Relations)

data.title: 11 Curator Hotel & Resort Collection Properties Ranked in the Prestigious Condé Nast Traveler 2025 Readers’ Choice Awards
data.description:
11 Curator Hotel & Resort Collection Properties Ranked in the Prestigious Condé Nast Traveler 2025 Readers’ Choice Awards

As independent hotels continue to redefine hospitality through creativity, innovation, and personalized service, Curator Hotel & Resort Collection is proud to celebrate 11 member properties honored in the Condé Nast Traveler’s 2025 Readers’ Choice Awards, leading the way in guest experiences and exceptional hospitality across the U.S.

The distinguished hotels and resorts were voted among this year's top selections by travelers around the world, with Curator members recognized as the best stays in San Francisco, Washington, D.C., the Pacific Northwest, the Midwest, Southern California, Los Angeles, and the Florida Keys.

“Independent hotels continue to play an important role in today’s travel industry, standing out because of their ability to personalize the guest experience, create genuine connections and memorable moments that last beyond a stay,” said Jennifer Barnwell, President of Curator Hotel & Resort Collection. “We’re proud to see how travelers continue to recognize and celebrate not only our members but independent hotels across the U.S. and internationally. Through Curator, we’re committed to providing independent hotels with the support and tools they need to continue to lead the industry.”

The following Curator members were recognized in the 2025 Condé Nast Readers’ Choice Awards across the following categories:

  • Top 10 Hotels in San Francisco
    • #2: Hotel Zephyr San Francisco
    • #3: Hotel Zelos San Francisco
    • #4: Hotel Zeppelin San Francisco
  • Top 10 Hotels in Washington, DC
    • #1: Hotel Zena Washington, DC
    • #5: Viceroy Washington, D.C.
  • Top 10 Hotels in the Pacific Northwest
    • #6: Salishan Coastal Lodge by SCP Hotels
  • Top 15 Hotels in the Midwest
    • #7: The Fontaine
  • Top 10 Resorts in the Florida Keys
    • #2: The Marker Harbor Resort
    • #9: Little Palm Island Resort & Spa
  • Top 20 Hotels in Southern California
    • #5: L’Auberge Del Mar
  • Top 20 Hotels in Los Angeles
    • #10: Los Angeles Athletic Club

About Curator Hotel & Resort Collection

Curator Hotel & Resort Collection is a distinct collection of small brands and independent lifestyle hotels and resorts worldwide. Curator provides lifestyle hotels and resorts access to a wide breadth of program offerings to enhance the guest experience, improve employee engagement, provide additional marketing support, and drive incremental revenue, all resulting in value creation while allowing them to retain what makes them unique. Curator offers the benefits of associating with other unique lifestyle hotels, resorts, and small brands while participating in best-in-class operating agreements, services, reporting, and technology.

For more information, visit www.curatorhotelsandresorts.com and follow @CuratorHotelsResorts.
